output.sparql:
PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
PREFIX pq: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/>
PREFIX wd: <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/>
PREFIX p: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/>
PREFIX ps: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/>
SELECT ?actor WHERE {
  wd:Q4525 p:P161 ?statement .
  ?statement ps:P161 ?actor ; pq:P453 ?character .
  ?character rdfs:label ?label .
  FILTER regex(?label , "Ducky" , "i")
}
output.selections:
Using entities:
- NCIS (wd:Q4525), also known as "Navy NCIS", "NCIS: Naval Criminal Investigative Service":
  - American military drama/police procedural fiction television series (2003-)
  - is instance of television series

Using properties:
- cast member (wd:P161 as p/ps), also known as "actor", "actress", "contestant of a play", "feat.", "film starring", etc.:
  - actor in the subject production [use "character role" (P453) ... "recorded participant" (P11108) for non-fiction productions]
- character role (wd:P453 as pq), also known as "acting role", "as", "performer of", "plays", "role":
  - specific role played or filled by subject -- use only as qualifier of "cast member" (P161), "voice actor" (P725)
output.result:
Got 1 row and 1 column:
| actor                       |
| --------------------------- |
| David McCallum (wd:Q313023) |
output.answer: The actor who plays Ducky on NCIS is David McCallum.
output.formatted:
The actor who plays Ducky on NCIS is David McCallum.

SPARQL query over wikidata:
```sparql
PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
PREFIX pq: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/>
PREFIX wd: <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/>
PREFIX p: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/>
PREFIX ps: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/>
SELECT ?actor WHERE {
  wd:Q4525 p:P161 ?statement .
  ?statement ps:P161 ?actor ; pq:P453 ?character .
  ?character rdfs:label ?label .
  FILTER regex(?label , "Ducky" , "i")
}
```

Using entities:
- NCIS (wd:Q4525), also known as "Navy NCIS", "NCIS: Naval Criminal Investigative Service":
  - American military drama/police procedural fiction television series (2003-)
  - is instance of television series

Using properties:
- cast member (wd:P161 as p/ps), also known as "actor", "actress", "contestant of a play", "feat.", "film starring", etc.:
  - actor in the subject production [use "character role" (P453) ... "recorded participant" (P11108) for non-fiction productions]
- character role (wd:P453 as pq), also known as "acting role", "as", "performer of", "plays", "role":
  - specific role played or filled by subject -- use only as qualifier of "cast member" (P161), "voice actor" (P725)

Execution result:
Got 1 row and 1 column:
| actor                       |
| --------------------------- |
| David McCallum (wd:Q313023) |

messages.content:
You are a question answering assistant. Your job is to generate a SPARQL query to answer a given user question.

You should follow a step-by-step approach to generate the SPARQL query:
1. Determine possible entities and properties implied by the user question.
2. Search for the entities and properties in the knowledge graphs. Where applicable, constrain the searches with already identified entities and properties.
3. Gradually build up the SPARQL query using the identified entities and properties. Start with simple queries and add more complexity as needed. Execute intermediate queries to get feedback and to verify your assumptions. You may need to refine or rethink your current plan based on the query results and go back to step 2 if needed, possibly multiple times.
4. Use the answer or cancel function to finalize your answer and stop the generation process.

Available knowledge graphs:
- "wikidata" at https://qlever.dev/api/wikidata
  - "entity" index (type="fuzzy"): Entities indexed by their labels and synonyms
  - "property" index (type="embedding", modalities="text"): Properties indexed by their labels, synonyms, and identifiers

Index types used:
- "embedding": Retrieves items by cosine similarity between their embeddings and the query embedding. The embedding model used depends on the index and may support text, images, or both.
- "fuzzy": Retrieves items by overlap between their label words and the query keywords. The query keywords must not match label words exactly, but some fuzziness is allowed. The longer a query keyword is, the more it can deviate from a label word and still be considered a match, though it will also contribute less to the overall score. No special query operators like AND/OR are supported.

SPARQL prefixes for use in function calls:
- bd: http://www.bigdata.com/rdf#
- cc: http://creativecommons.org/ns#
- data: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityData/

- dct: http://purl.org/dc/terms/
- foaf: http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/
- geo: http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#
- geof: http://www.opengis.net/def/function/geosparql/
- gn: http://www.geonames.org/ontology#
- hint: http://www.bigdata.com/queryHints#

- p: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/
- pq: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/

- qlss: https://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/spatialSearch/
- rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#
- rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#
- ref: http://www.wikidata.org/reference/

- wdtn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct-normalized/
- wikibase: http://wikiba.se/ontology#
- xsd: http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#

Additional rules to follow:
- Explain your thought process before and after each step and function call.
- Do not ask the user for clarification until you have made an initial attempt at completing the task. When the task input is incomplete or ambiguous, proceed based on reasonable assumptions.
- Do not use "SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language ..." in SPARQL queries. It is unsupported by the used QLever SPARQL endpoints. Use rdfs:label or similar properties to get labels instead.
- Always execute your final SPARQL query before giving an answer to make sure it returns the expected results.
- The SPARQL query should always return the actual identifiers / IRIs of the items in its result. It additionally may return labels or other human-readable information, but they are optional and should be put within optional clauses unless explicitly requested by the user.
- Do not stop early if there are still obvious improvements to be made to the SPARQL query. For example, keep refining your SPARQL query if its result contains irrelevant items or is missing items you expected.
- Do not perform additional computation (e.g. filtering, sorting, calculations) on the result of the SPARQL query to determine the answer. All computation should be done solely within SPARQL.
- For questions with a "True" or "False" answer the SPARQL query should be an ASK query.

tool_calls.result:
Top 10 property alternatives:
1. name of the character role (wd:P4633 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "character name"), also known as "acting role", "as", "character name", "character named as", "character role named as", etc.:
  - name by which a character role is recorded in a database or ... a specific performance of a theatrical production of a play
2. present in work (wd:P1441 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "character in"), also known as "appeared in", "appears in", "appears in work", "character in", "featured in work", etc.:
  - this (fictional or fictionalized) entity, place, or person ... P1343 for entities described in non-fictional accounts)
3. label in sign language (wd:P2919 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "name sign"), also known as "name in sign language", "name sign", "sign", "sign language media", "sign name":
  - media file showing label of this item in sign language. Use ... work or name" (P407) as qualifier to indicate which language
4. character role (wd:P453 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "acting role", "as", "performer of", "plays", "role":
  - specific role played or filled by subject -- use only as qualifier of "cast member" (P161), "voice actor" (P725)
5. character type (wd:P9071 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "archetype", "character archetype", "character stereotype", "-dere", "dere", etc.:
  - generic character type that this fictional character represents
6. given name (wd:P735 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "Christian name"), also known as "Christian name", "first name", "firstname", "forename", "middle name", etc.:
  - first name or another given name of this person; values used with the property should not link disambiguations nor family names
7. author given names (wd:P9687 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "given name"), also known as "author first name", "author first names", "first name", "given name", "given names":
  - qualifier for string representation of given or secondary ... in a bibliographic reference file (for example BibTeX)
8. official name (wd:P1448 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "name"), also known as "be named", "complete title", "full title", "legal name", "long name", etc.:
  - official name of the subject in its official language(s)
9. name (wd:P2561 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "aka", "also known as", "be known as", "be referred as", "known as", etc.:
  - name the subject is known by; preferably use a more specific subproperty if available
10. self-identified name (wd:P12089 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "own name"), also known as "endonym", "internal name", "own name":
  - name by which a group of humans refers to itself (endonym)

Next, they looked for the property "character name" and found P4633 as "name of the character role". But I think the key here is to find the actor associated with the character Ducky in NCIS.

In Wikidata, for TV shows, the cast members are often listed using the "cast member" (P161) property, and each cast member has a "character name" (P4633) qualifier. So, the approach would be:

1. Identify the NCIS TV series (which is wd:Q4525).
2. Look for statements with property P161 (cast member) on Q4525.
3. Check the qualifiers of those statements for the character name "Donald 'Ducky' Mallard" or similar.

wd:Q4525 wdt:P161 wd:ActorID .
wd:Q4525 pq:P4633 "Donald 'Ducky' Mallard".

  wd:Q4525 wdt:P161 ?actor .
  ?statement pq:P4633 wd:Qxxxx .  # But wait, character name might be a string, not an entity.
}
